title: Longest Repeated Substring
description: Find longest substring that appears at least twice

examples.explanation: n=5. Check L=4: substrings "abab" (i=0), check j=1: "baba" ≠ "abab"; "baba" (i=1), check j=2: "aba" ≠ "baba". No repeats for L=4. L=3: "aba" (i=0), check j=1: "bab" ≠ "aba"; j=2: "aba" == "aba" ✓ Found repeat at positions 0 and 2. Return "aba".
n=4. L=3: "aaa" (i=0), check j=1: "aaa" == "aaa" ✓ Found repeat. Return "aaa". Note: Longer substring "aaaa" length 4 doesn't repeat since it's the entire string and needs to appear at least twice.

approaches.name: Brute Force O(n^3)
approaches.summary: Check all possible substrings by iterating over start and end indices, and for each substring, scan the string to find a duplicate occurrence. Use this for small inputs or as a baseline to understand the problem.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Brute Force Longest Repeated Substring
ladders.desc: Complete O(n³) solution checking all possible substrings for duplicates

examples.trace: n=5. Check L=4: substrings "abab" (i=0), check j=1: "baba" ≠ "abab"; "baba" (i=1), check j=2: "aba" ≠ "baba". No repeats for L=4. L=3: "aba" (i=0), check j=1: "bab" ≠ "aba"; j=2: "aba" == "aba" ✓ Found repeat at positions 0 and 2. Return "aba".
n=4. L=3: "aaa" (i=0), check j=1: "aaa" == "aaa" ✓ Found repeat. Return "aaa". Note: Longer substring "aaaa" length 4 doesn't repeat since it's the entire string and needs to appear at least twice.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Check if Substring Repeats
ladders.desc: Function to verify if a given substring appears elsewhere in the string

examples.trace: target="aba", n=5. j starts at 1: compare s[1:4]="bab" with "aba": first char 'b'≠'a' → mismatch. j=2: compare s[2:5]="aba" with "aba": chars match at k=0,1,2 → exact match. Return true.
target="bc", n=4. j starts at 2: compare s[2:4]="cd" with "bc": first char 'c'≠'b' → mismatch. j=3: invalid since n-L=2, j≤2 only. No matches. Return false.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Iterate All Substrings from Longest to Shortest
ladders.desc: Generate all possible substrings in decreasing length order
ladders.explanation:
This core logic forms the outer structure of the brute force solution. The key insight is that to find the longest repeating substring efficiently within the brute force paradigm, we should check substrings from longest possible down to shortest. This way, the first repeat we find is guaranteed to be the longest, allowing early termination. The intuition is that if a longer substring repeats, we don't need to check shorter ones. The algorithm iterates length L from n-1 down to 1. For each L, it generates all substrings of that length by varying the start index i from 0 to n-L. This produces substrings like: for L=n-1: s[0:n-1], s[1:n]; for L=n-2: s[0:n-2], s[1:n-1], s[2:n]; etc. Each substring is defined by its start and end indices. The time complexity for this generation alone is O(n²) since we have nested loops over L and i. Space complexity is O(1) as we only store indices. This connects to L1 by providing each generated substring to the duplicate checking subroutine. Common mistakes include incorrect loop bounds that miss some substrings or cause index errors, and not decrementing L properly. The intuition for starting with longest substrings is optimization: we want the maximum length, so checking longest first minimizes work. Real pattern: two nested loops, outer decreasing length, inner increasing start.

Pseudocode:
for L from n-1 down to 1:
    for i from 0 to n-L:
        substring = s[i:i+L]
        # Pass to duplicate check

Time: O(n²), Space: O(1)

examples.trace: n=3. L=2: i=0 → "ab", i=1 → "bc". L=1: i=0 → "a", i=1 → "b", i=2 → "c". Total 5 substrings checked from longest to shortest.

testCases.expected: L=3: abc,bcd; L=2: ab,bc,cd; L=1: a,b,c,d
L=5: xyzxy,yzxyz; L=4: xyzx,yzxy,zxyz; L=3: xyz,yzx,zxy,xyz; L=2: xy,yz,zx,xy,yz; L=1: x,y,z,x,y,z
L=7: abcdefg,bcdefgh; L=6: abcdef,bcdefg,cdefgh; ... continues to L=1
L=3: tes,est; L=2: te,es,st; L=1: t,e,s,t

ladders.title: Building Block — Extract Substring by Indices
ladders.desc: Get a substring from a string using start and end indices
ladders.explanation:
This building block is fundamental to working with substrings. Given a string s and two indices i (start) and j (end, exclusive), the task is to extract the substring s[i:j]. The intuition is that strings are sequences of characters, and a substring is a contiguous segment defined by its position bounds. The algorithm simply copies characters from position i to j-1 inclusive into a new string. In implementation, this is typically done with built-in slicing, but understanding the manual process is key. We iterate from index i to j-1, appending each character to a result string. Time complexity is O(L) where L=j-i is the substring length. Space complexity is O(L) for the new string. This connects to L2 as each generated substring in the iteration needs to be extracted for processing. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors (using j inclusive instead of exclusive), handling invalid indices, and not understanding that slicing creates a new copy. The intuition behind exclusive end index is consistency with half-open intervals [i,j), which simplifies calculations. Real pattern: build result character by character or use slicing.

Pseudocode:
function substring(s, i, j):  # j exclusive
    result = ""
    for k from i to j-1:
        result += s[k]
    return result

Time: O(L), Space: O(L)

examples.trace: result starts empty. k=1: s[1]='e' → result="e". k=2: s[2]='l' → result="el". k=3: s[3]='l' → result="ell". k=4 is j, stop. Return "ell".
k=2: 'c' → "c". k=3: 'd' → "cd". k=4: 'e' → "cde". Stop at k=5. Return "cde".

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Compare Two Strings Character by Character
ladders.desc: Check if two strings are exactly equal by comparing each character

examples.trace: Lengths both 3. i=0: 'a'=='a' ✓. i=1: 'b'=='b' ✓. i=2: 'c'=='c' ✓. All match. Return true.
Lengths both 5. i=0: 'h'=='h' ✓. i=1: 'e'=='e' ✓. i=2: 'l'=='l' ✓. i=3: 'l'!='p' → mismatch. Return false immediately.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Access Characters in a String by Index
ladders.desc: Retrieve a single character from a string using its position
ladders.explanation:
This is the most basic prerequisite for string manipulation. A string is a sequence of characters, and each character has an index starting from 0. The intuition is that strings are like arrays of characters, and we can access any character if we know its position. The operation is straightforward: given a string s and an index i, return the character at that position. In most programming languages, this is done with s[i] notation. It's important to understand that indices must be within bounds: 0 ≤ i < length(s). Accessing out of bounds causes errors. Time complexity is O(1) for direct access. Space complexity is O(1) as we just return a character. This connects to L4 as character comparison requires accessing characters from both strings. Common mistakes include using 1-based indexing (common in math but not programming), forgetting that strings are immutable (access doesn't change them), and not checking bounds. The intuition for zero-based indexing is historical in computing and simplifies calculations with offsets. Real pattern: use index operator.

Pseudocode:
function getChar(s, i):
    return s[i]  # Ensure i is valid

Time: O(1), Space: O(1)

examples.trace: String "hello" has indices: 0:'h', 1:'e', 2:'l', 3:'l', 4:'o'. At i=1, character is 'e'.
Indices: 0:'a', 1:'b', 2:'c'. i=0 gives 'a'.

approaches.name: Dynamic Programming O(n^2)
approaches.summary: Use a 2D DP table where dp[i][j] stores the length of the longest common suffix for substrings ending at indices i and j, with i != j to ensure non-overlapping or distinct occurrences. Track the maximum value to find the longest repeated substring.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Longest Repeated Substring using DP O(n²)
ladders.desc: Complete solution finding longest substring appearing at least twice using 2D DP table.
ladders.explanation: This solution uses dynamic programming to find the longest substring that appears at least twice in a given string. The key insight is that we can track common suffixes between all pairs of positions in the string. We create a 2D DP table where dp[i][j] represents the length of the longest common suffix for substrings ending at indices i and j. For i ≠ j, if characters at positions i and j match, then dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j-1] + 1, otherwise dp[i][j] = 0. This works because if two substrings share a common suffix, extending them by one character either continues the common suffix or breaks it. We track the maximum dp[i][j] value where i ≠ j to find the longest repeated substring. The time complexity is O(n²) because we fill an n×n table, and space complexity is O(n²) for storing the table. The connection to L1 is that this level implements the complete algorithm using the DP table construction from L1. Common mistakes include forgetting to ensure i ≠ j (which would count overlapping substrings) and not properly handling base cases for i=0 or j=0. The real code pattern initializes dp as a 2D array of zeros, iterates through all i,j pairs, updates dp[i][j] based on character matches, and tracks the maximum length and its ending index to reconstruct the substring.

examples.trace:
String: banana (n=6)
Initialize dp[6][6] all zeros, max_len=0, max_end=0
Fill dp table:
i=0,j=1: chars 'b'≠'a' → dp[0][1]=0
...
i=2,j=4: chars 'n'='n' → dp[2][4]=dp[1][3]+1=0+1=1
...
i=3,j=5: chars 'a'='a' → dp[3][5]=dp[2][4]+1=1+1=2
...
i=4,j=2: chars 'n'='n' → dp[4][2]=dp[3][1]+1=0+1=1
...
i=5,j=3: chars 'a'='a' → dp[5][3]=dp[4][2]+1=1+1=2
Max found at dp[3][5]=2 → max_len=2, max_end=3
Substring from index 3-2+1=2 to 3: 'ana'

String: ababa (n=5)
Initialize dp[5][5] all zeros, max_len=0, max_end=0
Fill dp table:
i=0,j=2: chars 'a'='a' → dp[0][2]=0+1=1
...
i=1,j=3: chars 'b'='b' → dp[1][3]=dp[0][2]+1=1+1=2
...
i=2,j=4: chars 'a'='a' → dp[2][4]=dp[1][3]+1=2+1=3
Max found at dp[2][4]=3 → max_len=3, max_end=2
Substring from index 2-3+1=0 to 2: 'aba'

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — DP Table Construction for Common Suffixes
ladders.desc: Build the 2D DP table tracking longest common suffixes between all index pairs.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine constructs the core DP table that enables finding repeated substrings. The intuition is that repeated substrings manifest as common suffixes when comparing different positions in the string. For any two indices i and j (i ≠ j), if the characters match, the common suffix length increases by 1 from the previous positions (i-1, j-1); otherwise, it resets to 0. This creates a recurrence relation: dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j-1] + 1 if s[i] == s[j] else 0, with base cases dp[i][j] = 0 when i=0 or j=0. The algorithm initializes an n×n table with zeros, then iterates i from 0 to n-1 and j from 0 to n-1, applying the recurrence. Time complexity is O(n²) for filling the table, and space is O(n²) for storage. This feeds into L0 by providing the complete DP table that L0 uses to find the maximum value. Common mistakes include incorrect indexing (off-by-one errors) and not handling the i ≠ j condition properly. The real code pattern uses nested loops and conditional updates based on character equality.

examples.trace:
String: abab (n=4)
Initialize 4x4 table all zeros
Fill table:
i=0: j=0: skip (i=j), j=1: 'a'≠'b'→0, j=2: 'a'='a'→1, j=3: 'a'≠'b'→0
i=1: j=0: 'b'≠'a'→0, j=1: skip, j=2: 'b'≠'a'→0, j=3: 'b'='b'→1
i=2: j=0: 'a'='a'→1, j=1: 'a'≠'b'→0, j=2: skip, j=3: 'a'≠'b'→0
i=3: j=0: 'b'≠'a'→0, j=1: 'b'='b'→1, j=2: 'b'≠'a'→0, j=3: skip
Resulting table shows common suffix lengths.

String: aa (n=2)
Initialize 2x2 table all zeros
Fill table:
i=0: j=0: skip, j=1: 'a'='a'→1
i=1: j=0: 'a'='a'→1, j=1: skip
Table: [[0,1],[1,0]]

testCases.expected: Table with increasing values on anti-diagonals

ladders.title: Core Logic — Recurrence Relation for Common Suffix Length
ladders.desc: The recurrence dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j-1] + 1 if chars match else 0.
ladders.explanation: This core logic defines how we compute the length of common suffixes between substrings ending at i and j. The intuition is that if two substrings share a common suffix, then extending both by one character either continues the common suffix (if the new characters match) or breaks it (if they don't match). Mathematically, for i>0 and j>0, if s[i] == s[j], then the common suffix length at (i,j) is one more than at (i-1,j-1); otherwise, it's 0. Base cases: when i=0 or j=0, dp[i][j]=0 because there's no previous character to compare. This recurrence efficiently captures overlapping substructure—the solution for (i,j) depends only on (i-1,j-1). Time complexity for computing one value is O(1), and space is O(1) per cell. This feeds into L1 by providing the update rule used in table construction. Common mistakes include forgetting base cases or incorrectly applying the recurrence when i=j. The real code pattern is a simple conditional statement inside nested loops.

examples.trace:
Given s='abab', i=2 (char 'a'), j=0 (char 'a')
Check: s[2]=='a', s[0]=='a' → match
Since i>0 and j>0? i=2>0, j=0 not >0 → use base case? Actually j=0 is base case, but recurrence applies for i>0 AND j>0. Here j=0, so dp[2][0]=1 directly because chars match? Wait, correct: base case dp[i][j]=0 for i=0 or j=0, but here i=2, j=0, so j=0 → should be 0? Let's clarify: The recurrence is for i>0 AND j>0. For i=2, j=0, since j=0, it's a base case and dp[2][0]=1 if chars match? Actually in standard LCS suffix, dp[i][j] = 0 if i=0 or j=0. But in our problem, we want dp[2][0]=1 because 'a' matches 'a'. So correction: Base case: if i=0 or j=0, dp[i][j] = 1 if s[i]==s[j] else 0. Yes. So for i=2,j=0: chars match → dp[2][0]=1.

Given s='abab', i=3 (char 'b'), j=1 (char 'b'), prev_dp=dp[2][0]=1
Check: s[3]=='b', s[1]=='b' → match
Since i>0 and j>0 (3>0,1>0), use recurrence: dp[3][1] = dp[2][0] + 1 = 1+1=2? Wait prev_dp was given as 0? Let's recalc: Actually from example1, dp[2][0]=1. So dp[3][1] = dp[2][0] + 1 = 1+1=2. But output says 1? Error. Correct: dp[3][1] should be 2. So update: output=2.

ladders.title: Building Block — Comparing Substrings via Suffix Overlap
ladders.desc: Understanding how common suffixes indicate repeated substrings.
ladders.explanation: This building block establishes the relationship between repeated substrings and common suffixes. The intuition is that if a substring appears at least twice, then at the ending indices of those occurrences, the suffixes (ending parts) of the string starting from those indices will match up to the length of the repeated substring. For example, in 'banana', 'ana' repeats ending at indices 3 and 5; the suffixes 'ana' and 'ana' match for length 3. By comparing all pairs of indices via their suffixes, we can detect repetitions. The algorithm involves, for each pair (i,j) with i≠j, checking how many characters match going backwards from i and j until a mismatch or start of string. This naive approach would be O(n³), but DP optimizes it to O(n²). Time complexity for this concept is O(n) per pair if done naively, space O(1). This feeds into L2 by providing the conceptual basis for the recurrence. Common mistakes include confusing prefixes with suffixes or not handling overlapping indices. The real code pattern uses a while loop to compare characters moving leftwards.

examples.trace:
Compare suffixes ending at i=2 ('abab' index2='a') and j=0 ('abab' index0='a'):
Start: char at i=2='a', j=0='a' → match, length=1
Move left: i=1='b', j=-1 (out of bounds) → stop
Total common suffix length=1

Compare suffixes ending at i=5 ('banana' index5='a') and j=3 ('banana' index3='a'):
Step1: i=5='a', j=3='a' → match, len=1
Step2: i=4='n', j=2='n' → match, len=2
Step3: i=3='a', j=1='a' → match, len=3
Step4: i=2='n', j=0='b' → mismatch → stop
Total=3? Wait, but in L0 example for 'banana', dp[5][3]=2. Let's check: Actually i=5,j=3: chars 'a'='a' → len1, i=4,j=2: 'n'='n' → len2, i=3,j=1: 'a'='a' → len3, i=2,j=0: 'n'≠'b' → stop at len3. But dp[5][3] should be 2? Error in L0? In L0 trace: 'i=5,j=3: chars 'a'='a' → dp[5][3]=dp[4][2]+1=1+1=2'. So there's inconsistency. Let's correct: For 'banana', indices: 0=b,1=a,2=n,3=a,4=n,5=a. i=5,j=3: compare backwards: pos5='a', pos3='a' match; pos4='n', pos2='n' match; pos3='a', pos1='a' match; pos2='n', pos0='b' mismatch. So length=3. But DP might give 2 due to i≠j condition? Actually dp[5][3] uses dp[4][2], which is for i=4,j=2. Let's compute dp[4][2]: i=4,j=2: chars 'n'='n' → dp[4][2]=dp[3][1]+1. dp[3][1]: i=3,j=1: 'a'='a' → dp[3][1]=dp[2][0]+1. dp[2][0]: i=2,j=0: 'n'≠'b' → 0. So dp[3][1]=1, dp[4][2]=2, dp[5][3]=3. So L0 trace had error. Correct output for example should be 3. So update: output=3, trace shows length 3.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Character Comparison and Index Manipulation
ladders.desc: Comparing characters at indices and moving through string indices.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves accessing characters in a string by index and comparing them for equality. The intuition is that strings are sequences of characters, and we can use integer indices (0-based) to refer to positions. Comparing s[i] == s[j] checks if characters at positions i and j are the same. Index manipulation, such as i-1 to move to the previous character, is fundamental for traversing the string. The algorithm is straightforward: given indices i and j, if they are within bounds (0 ≤ i,j < n), access s[i] and s[j] and compare using equality operator. Time complexity O(1), space O(1). This feeds into L3 by providing the mechanism for suffix comparison. Common mistakes include index out-of-bounds errors or confusing 0-based with 1-based indexing. The real code pattern uses array indexing and comparison operators.

examples.trace:
String 'hello': indices 0='h',1='e',2='l',3='l',4='o'
i=1 → s[1]='e'
j=3 → s[3]='l'
Compare 'e' == 'l' → false

String 'hello': indices as above
i=2 → s[2]='l'
j=2 → s[2]='l'
Compare 'l' == 'l' → true

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Strings and Indices
ladders.desc: Understanding strings as sequences and accessing elements by index.
ladders.explanation: This foundation introduces strings as ordered collections of characters, where each character has a position called an index. In most programming languages, indices start at 0 (0-based indexing). For example, in string 'cat', index 0 is 'c', index 1 is 'a', index 2 is 't'. The length of a string is the number of characters, so 'cat' has length 3. Accessing a character at an index i is done with notation like s[i], which returns the character at that position. If i is out of range (e.g., i < 0 or i ≥ length), it causes an error. This concept is prerequisite because the DP approach relies on comparing characters at specific indices. Time complexity for access is O(1), space O(1). This feeds into L4 by providing the basic knowledge for character comparison. Common mistakes include using 1-based indexing or not checking bounds. The real code pattern involves declaring a string and using square brackets to access characters.

examples.trace:
String 'dog' has indices: 0='d', 1='o', 2='g'
Access s[1] → character at index 1 is 'o'

String 'apple' has indices: 0='a',1='p',2='p',3='l',4='e'
Access s[0] → 'a'
Access s[4] → 'e'

approaches.name: Suffix Array with LCP O(n log n)
approaches.summary: Build a suffix array of the string, then compute the longest common prefix (LCP) array between adjacent suffixes. The longest repeated substring corresponds to the maximum LCP value, as repeated substrings appear as common prefixes in sorted suffixes.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Longest Repeated Substring using Suffix Array with LCP
ladders.desc: Complete implementation to find the longest substring that appears at least twice in a string using suffix array and LCP array.
ladders.explanation:
This solution finds the longest repeated substring by leveraging the relationship between repeated substrings and common prefixes in sorted suffixes. The intuition is that any substring that appears multiple times in the original string will appear as a common prefix among multiple suffixes in the sorted suffix array. By sorting all suffixes and computing the longest common prefix between adjacent suffixes, we can efficiently find the maximum repeated substring.

Step-by-step algorithm:
1. Generate all suffixes of the input string S (length n)
2. Sort the suffixes lexicographically to create the suffix array
3. Compute the LCP array where LCP[i] = length of common prefix between suffix at SA[i] and SA[i-1]
4. Find the maximum value in the LCP array
5. The substring starting at SA[index_of_max] with length max_lcp is the answer

Time complexity: O(n log n) for sorting suffixes (using efficient suffix array construction)
Space complexity: O(n) for storing suffixes and arrays

Connection to level above: This is the complete solution that integrates all sub-components.

Common mistakes:
- Not handling empty strings or strings with no repeated substrings
- Off-by-one errors in LCP computation
- Inefficient suffix sorting that leads to O(n² log n) complexity

Real code pattern:
def longest_repeated_substring(s):
    n = len(s)
    # Build suffix array
    suffixes = [(s[i:], i) for i in range(n)]
    suffixes.sort(key=lambda x: x[0])
    sa = [idx for _, idx in suffixes]
    
    # Build LCP array
    lcp = [0] * n
    for i in range(1, n):
        j = sa[i-1]
        k = sa[i]
        while j < n and k < n and s[j] == s[k]:
            lcp[i] += 1
            j += 1
            k += 1
    
    # Find max LCP
    max_len = max(lcp)
    if max_len == 0:
        return ""
    max_idx = lcp.index(max_len)
    start = sa[max_idx]
    return s[start:start+max_len]

examples.trace:
Step 1: Generate suffixes with indices: [(banana,0), (anana,1), (nana,2), (ana,3), (na,4), (a,5)]
Step 2: Sort suffixes lexicographically: [(a,5), (ana,3), (anana,1), (banana,0), (na,4), (nana,2)]
Step 3: Build suffix array SA: [5,3,1,0,4,2]
Step 4: Compute LCP array:
  LCP[0]=0 (by definition)
  Compare SA[1]=3 (ana) and SA[0]=5 (a): common prefix 'a' length=1 → LCP[1]=1
  Compare SA[2]=1 (anana) and SA[1]=3 (ana): common prefix 'ana' length=3 → LCP[2]=3
  Compare SA[3]=0 (banana) and SA[2]=1 (anana): no common prefix → LCP[3]=0
  Compare SA[4]=4 (na) and SA[3]=0 (banana): no common prefix → LCP[4]=0
  Compare SA[5]=2 (nana) and SA[4]=4 (na): common prefix 'na' length=2 → LCP[5]=2
Step 5: Max LCP = 3 at index 2
Step 6: Longest repeated substring starts at SA[2]=1 with length 3: s[1:4] = 'ana'

Step 1: Generate 11 suffixes with indices
Step 2: Sort suffixes: [(i,10), (ippi,7), (issippi,4), (ississippi,1), (mississippi,0), (pi,9), (ppi,8), (sippi,6), (sissippi,3), (ssippi,5), (ssissippi,2)]
Step 3: SA: [10,7,4,1,0,9,8,6,3,5,2]
Step 4: Compute LCP:
  LCP[0]=0
  LCP[1]=1 (i)
  LCP[2]=1 (i)
  LCP[3]=4 (issi)
  LCP[4]=0
  LCP[5]=0
  LCP[6]=1 (p)
  LCP[7]=0
  LCP[8]=2 (si)
  LCP[9]=1 (s)
  LCP[10]=3 (ssi)
Step 5: Max LCP = 4 at index 3
Step 6: Substring starts at SA[3]=1, length 4: s[1:5] = 'issi'

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Building the LCP Array from Suffix Array
ladders.desc: Compute the Longest Common Prefix array given a string and its suffix array.
ladders.explanation:
The LCP array is crucial because it directly reveals repeated substrings. The intuition is that when suffixes are sorted, repeated substrings appear as common prefixes between adjacent entries. The LCP array stores the length of common prefix between each suffix and its predecessor in the sorted order.

Algorithm:
1. Initialize LCP array of length n with LCP[0] = 0
2. For i from 1 to n-1:
   a. Get starting indices: j = SA[i-1], k = SA[i]
   b. While characters match and within bounds: increment count
   c. Store count in LCP[i]

Time: O(n²) in naive implementation, but can be optimized to O(n) using Kasai's algorithm
Space: O(n) for the LCP array

Connection to level above: This LCP array provides the data needed to find the maximum repeated substring.

Common mistakes:
- Forgetting to handle string boundaries
- Not resetting counters properly between comparisons
- Confusing suffix indices with LCP indices

Real code pattern:
def build_lcp(s, sa):
    n = len(s)
    lcp = [0] * n
    for i in range(1, n):
        j = sa[i-1]
        k = sa[i]
        count = 0
        while j < n and k < n and s[j] == s[k]:
            count += 1
            j += 1
            k += 1
        lcp[i] = count
    return lcp

examples.trace:
Step 1: Initialize LCP = [0,0,0,0,0,0]
Step 2: i=1: Compare SA[0]=5 (a) and SA[1]=3 (ana): common 'a' length=1 → LCP[1]=1
Step 3: i=2: Compare SA[1]=3 (ana) and SA[2]=1 (anana): common 'ana' length=3 → LCP[2]=3
Step 4: i=3: Compare SA[2]=1 (anana) and SA[3]=0 (banana): no match → LCP[3]=0
Step 5: i=4: Compare SA[3]=0 (banana) and SA[4]=4 (na): no match → LCP[4]=0
Step 6: i=5: Compare SA[4]=4 (na) and SA[5]=2 (nana): common 'na' length=2 → LCP[5]=2
Final LCP: [0,1,3,0,0,2]

Step 1: Initialize LCP = [0,0,0,0,0]
Step 2: i=1: Compare SA[0]=4 (a) and SA[1]=2 (aba): common 'a' length=1 → LCP[1]=1
Step 3: i=2: Compare SA[1]=2 (aba) and SA[2]=0 (ababa): common 'aba' length=3 → LCP[2]=3
Step 4: i=3: Compare SA[2]=0 (ababa) and SA[3]=3 (ba): no match → LCP[3]=0
Step 5: i=4: Compare SA[3]=3 (ba) and SA[4]=1 (baba): common 'ba' length=2 → LCP[4]=2
Final LCP: [0,1,3,0,2]

ladders.title: Core Logic — Constructing Suffix Array for String Comparison
ladders.desc: Build a suffix array by sorting all suffixes of a string lexicographically.
ladders.explanation:
The suffix array is the backbone of this approach. The intuition is that sorting all suffixes brings similar substrings together, making repeated patterns easy to identify. When suffixes are sorted, any repeated substring will appear as a common prefix between adjacent or nearby suffixes in the sorted order.

Algorithm:
1. Generate all suffixes of the string with their starting indices
2. Sort the suffixes lexicographically
3. Extract the starting indices to form the suffix array

Time: O(n² log n) with naive sorting (comparing strings takes O(n) time)
Space: O(n²) if storing all suffixes explicitly, but can be optimized

Connection to level above: The suffix array enables efficient LCP computation by organizing suffixes in sorted order.

Common mistakes:
- Storing full suffixes instead of references
- Inefficient comparison during sort
- Not preserving original indices

Real code pattern:
def build_suffix_array(s):
    n = len(s)
    suffixes = []
    for i in range(n):
        suffixes.append((s[i:], i))
    suffixes.sort(key=lambda x: x[0])
    sa = [idx for _, idx in suffixes]
    return sa

examples.trace:
Step 1: Generate suffixes with indices:
  0: banana
  1: anana
  2: nana
  3: ana
  4: na
  5: a
Step 2: Sort suffixes lexicographically:
  a (index 5)
  ana (index 3)
  anana (index 1)
  banana (index 0)
  na (index 4)
  nana (index 2)
Step 3: Extract indices: [5,3,1,0,4,2]

Step 1: Generate suffixes:
  0: abab
  1: bab
  2: ab
  3: b
Step 2: Sort lexicographically:
  ab (index 2)
  abab (index 0)
  b (index 3)
  bab (index 1)
Step 3: Extract indices: [2,0,3,1]

ladders.title: Building Block — Lexicographic Comparison of Strings
ladders.desc: Compare two strings character by character to determine their dictionary order.
ladders.explanation:
Lexicographic comparison is fundamental to sorting suffixes. The intuition is that strings can be ordered like words in a dictionary by comparing characters from left to right. This comparison determines the relative positions of suffixes in the sorted array.

Algorithm:
1. Initialize index i = 0
2. While both strings have characters at position i:
   a. If char1 < char2: return -1 (first comes before second)
   b. If char1 > char2: return 1 (first comes after second)
   c. If equal: increment i and continue
3. If all compared characters equal:
   a. If string1 is shorter: return -1 (shorter comes first)
   b. If string2 is shorter: return 1
   c. If equal length: return 0 (strings are identical)

Time: O(min(m,n)) where m,n are string lengths
Space: O(1)

Connection to level above: This comparison function is used by the sorting algorithm to order suffixes.

Common mistakes:
- Not handling different length strings correctly
- Off-by-one errors in loop conditions
- Returning wrong values for comparison results

Real code pattern:
def compare_strings(s1, s2):
    i = 0
    while i < len(s1) and i < len(s2):
        if s1[i] < s2[i]:
            return -1
        elif s1[i] > s2[i]:
            return 1
        i += 1
    if len(s1) < len(s2):
        return -1
    elif len(s1) > len(s2):
        return 1
    else:
        return 0

examples.trace:
Step 1: Compare first characters: 'a' vs 'b'
Step 2: 'a' < 'b' ASCII 97 < 98
Step 3: Return -1 (apple comes before banana)
No need to check further characters

Step 1: Compare 'c' vs 'c': equal
Step 2: Compare 'a' vs 'a': equal
Step 3: Compare 't' vs 't': equal
Step 4: String 'cat' ends, 'caterpillar' continues
Step 5: Shorter string comes first, return -1

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Extracting All Suffixes of a String
ladders.desc: Generate all possible suffixes (substrings starting at each position to end) of a given string.
ladders.explanation:
Suffixes are the building blocks for suffix array construction. The intuition is that any substring of the original string is a prefix of some suffix. By examining all suffixes, we indirectly examine all possible substrings.

Algorithm:
1. Initialize empty list for suffixes
2. For each index i from 0 to n-1:
   a. Extract substring from i to end of string
   b. Store this suffix along with its starting index i
3. Return the list of (suffix, index) pairs

Time: O(n²) if storing full copies, but can be O(n) with references
Space: O(n²) for storing all suffixes explicitly

Connection to level above: These suffixes are what get sorted to create the suffix array.

Common mistakes:
- Off-by-one errors in substring extraction
- Not preserving original indices
- Creating unnecessary copies of strings

Real code pattern:
def get_all_suffixes(s):
    n = len(s)
    suffixes = []
    for i in range(n):
        suffix = s[i:]  # substring from i to end
        suffixes.append((suffix, i))
    return suffixes

examples.trace:
Step 1: String length = 3
Step 2: i=0: s[0:] = 'abc' → ('abc',0)
Step 3: i=1: s[1:] = 'bc' → ('bc',1)
Step 4: i=2: s[2:] = 'c' → ('c',2)
Step 5: Return list with 3 suffix-index pairs

Step 1: String length = 5
Step 2: i=0: s[0:] = 'hello' → ('hello',0)
Step 3: i=1: s[1:] = 'ello' → ('ello',1)
Step 4: i=2: s[2:] = 'llo' → ('llo',2)
Step 5: i=3: s[3:] = 'lo' → ('lo',3)
Step 6: i=4: s[4:] = 'o' → ('o',4)
Step 7: Return list with 5 suffix-index pairs

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding String Substrings and Suffixes
ladders.desc: Learn what substrings and suffixes are, and how to extract them from a string.
ladders.explanation:
This foundational concept introduces the basic building blocks of string manipulation. A substring is any contiguous sequence of characters within a string. A suffix is a special type of substring that starts at some position and continues to the end of the string. Understanding these concepts is crucial because the longest repeated substring problem is essentially about finding substrings that repeat, and suffixes help us organize this search efficiently.

Key definitions:
- Substring: A contiguous sequence of characters from the original string
- Suffix: A substring that starts at position i and goes to the end of the string
- Prefix: A substring that starts at the beginning and goes to position i

Examples:
For string "banana":
- Substrings include: "b", "ba", "ban", "bana", "banan", "banana", "a", "an", etc.
- Suffixes: "banana" (from index 0), "anana" (index 1), "nana" (index 2), "ana" (index 3), "na" (index 4), "a" (index 5)

Why this matters: Any repeated substring in the original string will appear as a common prefix in two or more suffixes. This insight transforms the problem from searching all possible substrings to examining relationships between suffixes.

Common misunderstandings:
- Confusing substrings with subsequences (which don't need to be contiguous)
- Thinking suffixes must be unique (they can repeat)
- Not realizing that every substring is a prefix of some suffix

Real code pattern:
# Get a substring from index i to j-1
def get_substring(s, i, j):
    return s[i:j]

# Get suffix starting at index i
def get_suffix(s, i):
    return s[i:]

examples.input: String: cat, List all substrings of length 2
examples.trace:
Step 1: String "cat" has length 3
Step 2: Possible starting positions for length 2 substrings: 0 and 1
Step 3: Starting at 0: s[0:2] = "ca"
Step 4: Starting at 1: s[1:3] = "at"
Step 5: Total 2 substrings of length 2

Step 1: String "hello" has length 5
Step 2: Suffix starting at 0: s[0:] = "hello"
Step 3: Suffix starting at 1: s[1:] = "ello"
Step 4: Suffix starting at 2: s[2:] = "llo"
Step 5: Suffix starting at 3: s[3:] = "lo"
Step 6: Suffix starting at 4: s[4:] = "o"
Step 7: Total 5 suffixes

testCases.input: String: abc, Get substring from index 1 to 3
String: programming, Get suffix starting at index 3
String: test, Get substring from index 0 to 2
String: algorithm, Get suffix starting at index 4
String: structure, List all substrings of length 3 starting at index 0, 2, 4
String: python, Get suffix starting at index 1
String: education, Get substring from index 2 to 6
String: computer, List all suffixes starting with 'c'
String: science, Get the last 3 characters as a substring

approaches.name: Rolling Hash with Binary Search O(n log n)
approaches.summary: Use binary search on the length of the substring to find the maximum length where a duplicate exists, and for each length, compute rolling hashes to check for duplicates in O(n) time via a hash set. This optimizes by avoiding checking all lengths explicitly.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Longest Repeated Substring with Rolling Hash and Binary Search
ladders.desc: Complete O(n log n) solution using binary search on substring length and rolling hash detection.
ladders.explanation: This solution efficiently finds the longest substring that appears at least twice by combining binary search with rolling hashes. The intuition is that if a substring of length L appears at least twice, then all shorter lengths < L also have duplicates, creating a monotonic property perfect for binary search. For each candidate length mid in binary search, we compute rolling hashes of all substrings of that length in O(n) time using polynomial rolling hash with modulo operations. By storing hashes in a hash set, we can detect duplicates in O(1) average time. The algorithm maintains the longest valid length found. Time complexity is O(n log n) for n binary search iterations each with O(n) hash computation. Space is O(n) for storing hashes. Connection to L1: This integrates the duplicate detection subroutine with binary search framework. Common mistakes include hash collisions (mitigated with double hashing), improper modulo arithmetic causing overflow, and off-by-one errors in binary search bounds. Real pattern: binary search over [0, n], compute rolling hashes for length mid, check duplicates, adjust search range.

examples.trace: 1. Binary search: low=0, high=5, mid=2. Check length 2: compute rolling hashes: 'ab': hash1, 'ba': hash2, 'ab': hash1 (duplicate found). Update answer=2, low=3. 2. low=3, high=5, mid=4. Check length 4: 'abab': hash3, 'baba': hash4 (no duplicates). high=3. 3. low=3, high=3, mid=3. Check length 3: 'aba': hash5, 'bab': hash6 (no duplicates). high=2. Search ends. Longest length=2, but need actual substring: from duplicate hash1 at indices 0 and 2, substring 'ab' length 2, but check longer: actually 'aba' appears at indices 0 and 2 (overlap allowed), so final output 'aba'.

1. Binary search: low=0, high=5, mid=2. Check length 2: 'aa' appears multiple times (duplicate). Update answer=2, low=3. 2. low=3, high=5, mid=4. Check length 4: 'aaaa' appears at indices 0 and 1 (duplicate). Update answer=4, low=5. 3. low=5, high=5, search ends. Longest length=4, substring 'aaaa' from indices 0-3.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Detect Duplicate Substrings of Fixed Length Using Rolling Hash
ladders.desc: Check if any substring of given length L appears at least twice in O(n) time.
ladders.explanation: This subroutine determines whether there exists a duplicate substring of exact length L in the string. The intuition uses rolling hash to efficiently compute hashes for all substrings of length L in linear time. Starting from the first L characters, compute initial hash using polynomial hash: hash = (s[0]*p^(L-1) + s[1]*p^(L-2) + ... + s[L-1]) mod M. Then slide window: new_hash = ((old_hash - s[start]*p^(L-1))*p + s[end]) mod M. Store each hash in a hash set; if a hash is already present, a duplicate exists (with collision handling). Time O(n) for n-L+1 substrings, space O(n) for hash set. Connection to L0: This is called for each mid in binary search. Common mistakes: not using modulo properly leading to overflow, forgetting to handle negative values in modulo, and not considering hash collisions. Real pattern: compute initial hash, iterate through string updating hash, track seen hashes.

examples.trace: 1. Compute rolling hashes for length 2: start at index 0: 'ab' hash1. 2. Index 1: 'ba' hash2. 3. Index 2: 'ab' hash1 (duplicate of hash1 found). Return true.
1. Compute hashes: index 0 'abc' hash1, index 1 'bcd' hash2, index 2 'cde' hash3. All unique. Return false.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Binary Search on Answer for Optimization
ladders.desc: Use binary search to find maximum length with duplicate substring efficiently.
ladders.explanation: The core insight is that the property 'has duplicate substring of length L' is monotonic: if length L has a duplicate, then all shorter lengths < L also have duplicates (since a substring of a duplicate is not necessarily duplicate, but we consider non-overlapping or careful handling; actually for this problem, if a longer duplicate exists, a shorter one might not, but we use binary search on the existence function which is monotonic if we define it properly). We define function f(L) that returns true if there exists duplicate substring of length L. Observe that if f(L) is true, then f(L-1) might not be true, but for binary search we search for maximum L where f(L) is true. We initialize low=0, high=n (string length). While low < high, compute mid = (low+high+1)//2. If f(mid) is true, set low=mid (since we want maximum), else high=mid-1. After loop, low is the maximum length. Time O(log n) iterations. Connection to L1: f(L) is implemented by the duplicate detection subroutine. Common mistakes: off-by-one in binary search bounds, incorrect mid calculation leading to infinite loops, misunderstanding monotonicity. Real pattern: standard binary search for maximum valid value.

examples.input: String length 5, f(L) results: f(0)=true, f(1)=true, f(2)=true, f(3)=false, f(4)=false, f(5)=false
examples.trace: 1. low=0, high=5, mid=3. f(3)=false, so high=2. 2. low=0, high=2, mid=1. f(1)=true, so low=1. 3. low=1, high=2, mid=2. f(2)=true, so low=2. 4. low=2, high=2, stop. Maximum length=2.

examples.input: String length 3, f(L): f(0)=true, f(1)=true, f(2)=true, f(3)=false
examples.trace: 1. low=0, high=3, mid=2. f(2)=true, so low=2. 2. low=2, high=3, mid=3. f(3)=false, so high=2. 3. low=2, high=2, stop. Length=2.

ladders.title: Building Block — Polynomial Rolling Hash Computation
ladders.desc: Compute hash of a string using polynomial rolling hash for O(1) updates.
ladders.explanation: Polynomial rolling hash allows efficient hash computation for substrings by representing string as coefficients in a polynomial. For a string s of length L, hash = (s[0]*p^(L-1) + s[1]*p^(L-2) + ... + s[L-1]) mod M, where p is a prime base (e.g., 31, 53) and M is a large prime modulus (e.g., 10^9+7). The intuition is that each character is weighted by a power of p, creating a unique fingerprint. To update hash when sliding window: new_hash = ((old_hash - s[start]*p^(L-1))*p + s[end]) mod M. Precompute powers of p modulo M for O(1) access. Time O(1) per update after preprocessing, space O(n) for power array. Connection to L2: This enables O(n) duplicate detection. Common mistakes: integer overflow (use modulo operations carefully), choosing poor p or M leading to collisions, not handling negative values in modulo. Real pattern: init hash, update via formula, use modulo arithmetic.

examples.input: String 'abc', p=31, M=1e9+7, compute hash for length 2 starting at index 0
Update from 'ab' to 'bc' in 'abc', old hash 3105, p=31, M=1e9+7

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Modulo Arithmetic for Large Numbers
ladders.desc: Perform modulo operations to prevent integer overflow in hash computations.
ladders.explanation: Modulo arithmetic is essential when dealing with large numbers in rolling hash to avoid integer overflow. The operation a mod M returns the remainder when a is divided by M. Key properties: (a+b) mod M = ((a mod M) + (b mod M)) mod M, (a*b) mod M = ((a mod M) * (b mod M)) mod M. This allows computation in bounded range. Intuition: by taking modulo at each step, we keep numbers small while preserving the hash distribution. In rolling hash, we compute hash = (hash * p + char_code) mod M for updates. Time O(1) per operation. Connection to L3: This ensures hash values stay within integer limits. Common mistakes: forgetting to apply modulo after each operation, negative results from subtraction (fix by adding M before modulo), using floating-point numbers. Real pattern: use % operator, handle negatives with (x % M + M) % M.

examples.input: Update hash: old_hash=500000000, p=31, char=97, M=1000000007

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — String Substrings and Duplicate Detection
ladders.desc: Understand substrings and check for duplicates using basic comparison.
ladders.explanation: A substring is a contiguous sequence of characters within a string. For a string of length n, there are n*(n+1)/2 possible substrings. To find if a substring appears at least twice, we can generate all substrings and compare them. Intuition: by examining all possible substrings, we can directly check for duplicates, though this is inefficient (O(n^3) with naive comparison). For example, in string 'ababa', substrings include 'a', 'ab', 'aba', etc., and 'aba' appears at indices 0 and 2. Step-by-step: iterate start index from 0 to n-1, end index from start to n-1, extract substring, store in a set, check if already seen. Time O(n^3) for generation and comparison, space O(n^3) for storing substrings. Connection to L4: This foundational concept leads to optimized methods like hashing. Common mistakes: off-by-one in indices, confusion between substring and subsequence, not considering overlapping occurrences. Real pattern: nested loops, use set for duplicates.

examples.trace: 1. Substrings: start=0, end=0: 'a'. start=0, end=1: 'ab'. start=1, end=1: 'b'. Store in set: {'a','ab','b'}. All unique, no duplicates.
1. Substrings: start=0, end=0: 'a'. start=0, end=1: 'aa'. start=1, end=1: 'a'. Set: after 'a', 'aa', then 'a' already in set, so duplicate found: 'a' appears twice.
